1:
"Men, brethren, and fathers, hear ye my defence which I make now unto you." The witnesses note Paul's courteous and humble address to his persecutors. Gill marks the remarkableness: that the apostle should address in this conciliatory manner those who had just dragged him from the temple and beaten him unmercifully, when they scarcely deserved the name of "men," yet he calls them "brethren" (his countrymen and kinsmen) and "fathers" (the elders among them); "this shows how ready the apostle was to put up with affronts, and to forgive injuries done him." The witnesses note Paul, beginning his defence, addressing the hostile mob with respect and meekness. The witnesses note the courteous address. The witnesses note Paul's meekness.

The witnesses note Paul's courteous, humble address to his persecutors — "brethren and fathers" — his meekness and readiness to forgive.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's courteous address. The lesson the expositors draw is the meekness of the apostle, addressing his persecutors with respect and a desire to win them. The witnesses note the address: "Men, brethren, and fathers" — Paul addresses the mob with the customary respectful form; Gill marks the remarkableness — these were the very men who had just dragged him from the temple and beaten him, scarcely deserving the name of "men," yet Paul calls them brethren (his countrymen) and fathers (the elders); the courtesy to his persecutors. The expositors observe the meekness this displays: Paul, unjustly assaulted, does not revile his attackers, but addresses them with respect and tenderness; the readiness to put up with affronts and forgive injuries; the meekness that returns courtesy for cruelty; the spirit of Christ in the apostle. The witnesses press the purpose of the conciliation: "hear ye my defence which I make now unto you" — Paul seeks to be heard, to make his defence; the courteous address calculated to win their attention and goodwill, that he might clear himself and preach Christ; the wisdom that conciliates to gain a hearing. The expositors draw the lesson on meekness and wisdom: Paul's address joins meekness (forgiving his persecutors) and wisdom (conciliating to gain a hearing); the apostle's spirit and prudence; "be ye angry, and sin not"; the meek and wise approach to the hostile. The witnesses observe the example for the persecuted: Paul, beaten and hated, addresses his enemies as brethren and fathers; the example of returning good for evil, of seeking the good of persecutors; the believer to bless them that curse (Matthew 5:44); the meekness that wins. The expositors note the desire to win his countrymen: Paul's defence is not merely self-justification, but an opportunity to preach Christ to his people; the heart that longs for their salvation (Romans 9:1-3); the defence a witness; the meekness serving the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's courteous address, the meekness toward his persecutors, and the wisdom that conciliates to gain a hearing. The verse thus records Paul's courteous address — brethren and fathers, hear my defence — teaching the meekness of the apostle toward his persecutors and the wisdom that seeks to win them.
2:
"(And when they heard that he spake in the Hebrew tongue to them, they kept the more silence: and he saith,)" The witnesses note the effect of the Hebrew tongue in quieting the mob. Ellicott notes the opening words "had done the work they were meant to do" — one who spoke in Hebrew was not likely to blaspheme the sacred Hebrew books; "what follows was conceived in the same spirit of conciliation." Clarke notes Paul had probably been traduced as a mere Gentile, an enemy of the Jewish religion; speaking Hebrew refuted this. The witnesses note the mob, hearing Paul speak in the Hebrew (Aramaic) tongue, keeping the more silence — the wisdom of addressing them in their own sacred language, winning their attention. The witnesses note the Hebrew tongue. The witnesses note the mob's silence.

The witnesses note the mob quieted by Paul's Hebrew speech — the wisdom of addressing them in their own sacred tongue.

So the witnesses make the verse the effect of the Hebrew tongue. The lesson the expositors draw is the wisdom of conciliation, the mob quieted by Paul's speaking in their own sacred language. The witnesses note the effect: "when they heard that he spake in the Hebrew tongue to them, they kept the more silence" — the mob, hearing Paul speak in Hebrew (the Aramaic of the Jews), kept the more silence; the tongue itself winning their attention; the conciliation working. The expositors observe Ellicott's note: the opening words had done their work — one who spoke in Hebrew was not likely to blaspheme the sacred Hebrew books; the language itself disposing the mob to listen; the conciliation continued in the speech. The witnesses press Clarke's point on the refutation of the slander: Paul had probably been traduced by the Asian Jews as a mere Gentile, an enemy of the Jewish religion; his speaking Hebrew refuted this — showing him a Jew, conversant with the sacred tongue, not the Gentile enemy they supposed; the language clearing the charge. The expositors draw the lesson on the wisdom of conciliation: Paul addressed the mob in their own sacred tongue, winning their attention and disposing them to hear; the wisdom that meets men on their own ground, removes prejudice, gains a hearing; the prudence of the apostle. The witnesses observe the providence in the silence: the mob, lately raging, now kept silence; the providence quieting them, that Paul might speak; the opportunity given for the defence and the witness; the tumult stilled for the gospel. The expositors note the example of meeting men where they are: Paul, to win the Jews, spoke their language, appealed to their reverence for the Hebrew; the wisdom of accommodation in things indifferent, to gain a hearing for the truth; the becoming all things to all men (1 Corinthians 9:20). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Hebrew tongue quieting the mob, the wisdom of conciliation, and the providence giving the opportunity to speak. The verse thus records the effect of the Hebrew tongue — the mob keeping the more silence — teaching the wisdom of conciliation and the providence giving Paul a hearing.
3:
"I am verily a man which am a Jew, born in Tarsus, a city in Cilicia, yet brought up in this city at the feet of Gamaliel, and taught according to the perfect manner of the law of the fathers, and was zealous toward God, as ye all are this day." The witnesses note Paul's account of his Jewish extraction and education. Matthew Henry notes Paul gives such an account of himself as might satisfy the captain that he was not the Egyptian, and the Jews that he was not the enemy to their law and temple they supposed; his extraction and education — one of their own nation, of the stock of Israel. Gill notes he was a Jew by birth, a true Hebrew. The witnesses note Paul setting forth his Jewish birth, his education at the feet of Gamaliel in the strict law, and his zeal toward God — establishing his credentials as a true and zealous Jew. The witnesses note Paul's Jewish credentials. The witnesses note his education under Gamaliel.

The witnesses note Paul's Jewish credentials — born a Jew, taught by Gamaliel, zealous toward God — establishing his standing.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's account of his extraction and education. The lesson the expositors draw is the establishing of Paul's credentials as a true, learned, and zealous Jew. The witnesses note his birth: "a man which am a Jew, born in Tarsus, a city in Cilicia" — Paul was a Jew by birth, born in Tarsus (a notable city); Gill notes a true Hebrew, of the stock of Israel; his Jewish descent established. The expositors observe his education: "brought up in this city at the feet of Gamaliel, and taught according to the perfect manner of the law of the fathers" — Paul was reared in Jerusalem, educated at the feet of Gamaliel (the renowned teacher, Acts 5:34), taught strictly in the law of the fathers; his learning and orthodoxy established; a thorough, well-trained Jew. The witnesses press his zeal: "was zealous toward God, as ye all are this day" — Paul had been zealous for God, even as his hearers now were; his former zeal (which had made him a persecutor, v.4) matching theirs; the appeal to common ground — he had been as zealous as they. The expositors draw the lesson on Paul's establishing of his credentials, which Matthew Henry marks: Paul shows he was no Gentile enemy of the law and temple, but one of their own nation, learned in the law, zealous for God; refuting the slander, winning their sympathy; the credentials disposing them to hear. The witnesses observe the wisdom of the appeal to common ground: Paul shows he had been as zealous as they — a former persecutor of the Christians; meeting them on their own ground, that they might understand how great a change his conversion was; the appeal to their shared zeal. The expositors note the significance of his education under Gamaliel: Paul was no ignorant fanatic, but a trained scholar, taught by the greatest teacher; his learning and standing established; the apostle of no mean credentials. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's Jewish credentials, his education and zeal, and the wisdom of establishing his standing. The verse thus records Paul's account of his extraction and education — a Jew, taught by Gamaliel, zealous toward God — teaching the establishing of his credentials as a true, learned, and zealous Jew.
4:
"And I persecuted this way unto the death, binding and delivering into prisons both men and women." The witnesses note Paul's confession of his former persecution. Gill notes Paul persecuted the Christian religion and its professors, breathing out threatenings and slaughter, haling them from their houses, committing them to prison, consenting to their death; "a most bitter enemy, and an implacable persecutor"; which shows how great a work of divine power his conversion was. The witnesses note Paul confessing that he persecuted "this way" (the gospel) to the death, imprisoning men and women — establishing his former enmity, the foil to the grace that converted him. The witnesses note the former persecution. The witnesses note the foil to grace.

The witnesses note Paul confessing his former persecution of the gospel — the foil to the grace that converted him.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's confession of his former persecution. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth of Paul's former enmity, the foil to the grace that converted him. The witnesses note the confession: "I persecuted this way unto the death, binding and delivering into prisons both men and women" — Paul confesses that he had persecuted "this way" (the gospel, the Christian religion) to the death; Gill notes he breathed out threatenings and slaughter, haled the Christians from their houses, imprisoned them, consented to their death; a most bitter, implacable persecutor. The expositors observe the depth of his former enmity: Paul had been a violent persecutor, imprisoning men and women, consenting to death; the bitterness of his opposition; the enemy of the gospel; "I was before a blasphemer, and a persecutor, and injurious" (1 Timothy 1:13). The witnesses press the purpose of the confession: Paul confesses his former zeal against the gospel to establish how great a change his conversion was; the foil to the grace that converted the persecutor; the depth of his enmity magnifying the grace that overcame it; the change the proof of divine power. The expositors draw the lesson Gill marks: his former enmity shows how great a work of divine power his conversion was; only God could reconcile such a persecutor to the gospel, cause him to embrace and profess it; the conversion of the persecutor the triumph of grace; the depth of the sin the measure of the grace. The witnesses observe the appeal to his hearers: Paul shows he had been as zealous against the gospel as they now were; meeting them on their own ground; that they might understand his conversion was no light thing, but the work of God; the appeal to their shared former position. The expositors note the honesty of the confession: Paul does not hide his former sin, but confesses it openly; the honesty that owns the past, magnifies the grace; the penitent's confession; the persecutor turned apostle owning his crime. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's confession of his persecution, the depth of his former enmity, and the foil to the grace that converted him. The verse thus records Paul's confession of his former persecution — persecuting this way unto the death — teaching the depth of his enmity, the foil to the grace that converted the persecutor.
5:
"As also the high priest doth bear me witness, and all the estate of the elders: from whom also I received letters unto the brethren, and went to Damascus, to bring them which were there bound unto Jerusalem, for to be punished." The witnesses note the appeal to the witnesses of his former zeal. Gill notes the high priest (Annas or Caiaphas) could bear witness, and Paul may have kept the letter as a testimony. Barnes notes "all the estate of the elders" is the whole body of the sanhedrim; Paul received letters to the brethren (Jews) at Damascus. The witnesses note Paul appealing to the high priest and the council as witnesses of his former persecution, and recounting his commission to Damascus to bring the Christians bound to Jerusalem — the proof of his former zeal. The witnesses note the appeal to witnesses. The witnesses note the Damascus commission.

The witnesses note Paul appealing to the high priest and council as witnesses of his former zeal — his commission to Damascus.

So the witnesses make the verse the appeal to the witnesses of his former zeal. The lesson the expositors draw is the corroboration of Paul's former persecution by credible witnesses, establishing the truth of his account. The witnesses note the appeal: "the high priest doth bear me witness, and all the estate of the elders" — Paul appeals to the high priest (Annas or Caiaphas, Gill notes) and the whole sanhedrim (Barnes notes "all the estate of the elders" is the great council) as witnesses of his former zeal against the gospel; the credible witnesses who could confirm his account; the appeal to those who knew. The expositors observe the force of the appeal: Paul calls on the very authorities who had commissioned him to persecute — they could testify to his former enmity; the witnesses who could not deny it; the corroboration of his account by the highest authorities; the truth attested. The witnesses press the commission to Damascus: "from whom also I received letters unto the brethren, and went to Damascus, to bring them which were there bound unto Jerusalem, for to be punished" — Paul recounts his commission, the letters from the council to the Jews at Damascus, his journey to bring the Christians bound to Jerusalem for punishment; the proof of his former zeal; the violent mission against the gospel. The expositors draw the lesson on the establishing of his former zeal: Paul recounts his commission and journey to show the depth of his enmity, attested by the council; the violent persecutor, armed with letters, going to Damascus to imprison the Christians; the proof of his former position; the foil to the grace that met him on that very journey. The witnesses observe the wisdom of the appeal: Paul appeals to witnesses the Jews would credit — the high priest and council; the corroboration disposing them to believe his account of the conversion that followed; the credible witnesses lending weight to the testimony. The expositors note the dramatic contrast set up: Paul, armed with letters to persecute, on the way to Damascus, was met by Christ and converted; the persecutor on his mission turned; the contrast between his commission and his conversion; the grace that met the persecutor on his errand. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the appeal to the witnesses, the commission to Damascus, and the establishing of Paul's former zeal. The verse thus records the appeal to the witnesses of his former zeal — the high priest, the council, the Damascus commission — teaching the corroboration of his former persecution and the foil to the grace that converted him.
6:
"And it came to pass, that, as I made my journey, and was come nigh unto Damascus about noon, suddenly there shone from heaven a great light round about me." The witnesses note the Damascus road light, with the distinctive detail of noon. Barnes notes the circumstance "about noon" is omitted in the account in Acts 9; Paul mentions it as remarkable, since it occurred at midday, to show that he was not deluded by any meteoric or natural appearances (which usually occur at night). Ellicott marks the special note of the time. The witnesses note the great light from heaven shining round Paul at noon — the supernatural light, brighter than the noonday sun, that arrested the persecutor; the conversion beginning. The witnesses note the light at noon. The witnesses note the supernatural appearance.

The witnesses note the great light from heaven at noon — the supernatural appearance, brighter than the sun, that arrested Paul.

So the witnesses make the verse the Damascus road light. The lesson the expositors draw is the supernatural character of the light, attested by its shining at noon, arresting the persecutor. The witnesses note the appearance: "as I made my journey, and was come nigh unto Damascus about noon, suddenly there shone from heaven a great light round about me" — as Paul neared Damascus, at noon, a great light from heaven suddenly shone round him; the supernatural light arresting him on his errand of persecution. The expositors observe the distinctive detail of noon that Barnes marks: the circumstance "about noon" is omitted in Acts 9, but Paul mentions it here as remarkable — since it occurred at midday, it showed he was not deluded by any meteoric or natural appearance (which occur at night); the light shone at noon, brighter than the noonday sun (Acts 26:13); the supernatural character attested by the time. The witnesses press the supernatural character: a great light from heaven, at noon, brighter than the sun — no natural phenomenon, but a supernatural appearance; the glory of the risen Christ; the light that arrested the persecutor; the divine intervention. The expositors draw the lesson on the reality of the conversion: the light at noon proves the conversion was no delusion, no natural event, but the supernatural intervention of God; the persecutor arrested by the glory of Christ; the reality of the divine appearance; the conversion the work of God. The witnesses observe the sovereignty of the grace: Paul, on his errand of persecution, armed with letters (v.5), was arrested by the light from heaven; the persecutor stopped on his way; the sovereign grace that met him in the midst of his sin; the grace that arrests. The expositors note the providence in the timing: the light came at noon, on the journey, near Damascus — at the height of Paul's enmity, on his violent mission; the providence arresting him at the very point of his sin; the grace meeting the persecutor on his errand. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the great light at noon, the supernatural appearance, and the sovereign grace that arrested Paul. The verse thus records the Damascus road light at noon — the supernatural appearance brighter than the sun — teaching the supernatural character of the conversion and the sovereign grace that arrested the persecutor.
7:
"And I fell unto the ground, and heard a voice saying unto me, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?" The witnesses note Paul's fall and the voice from heaven. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the second aorist "I fell," the voice in the genitive (as Acts 9:7), and that Saul's name is repeated each time the account is given (Acts 9:4; 22:7; 26:14), with the same question. The witnesses note Paul falling to the ground and hearing the voice of the risen Christ — "Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?" — the searching question that identified the persecution of the church with the persecution of Christ himself. The witnesses note the fall. The witnesses note the searching question.

The witnesses note Paul falling and the voice — "why persecutest thou me?" — identifying the persecution of the church with Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's fall and the voice from heaven. The lesson the expositors draw is the searching question that identified the persecution of the church with the persecution of Christ himself. The witnesses note the fall: "I fell unto the ground" — Paul, arrested by the light, fell to the ground; the persecutor prostrate; Robertson notes the second aorist; the man brought down by the glory of Christ. The expositors observe the voice: "heard a voice saying unto me, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?" — the voice of the risen Christ, calling Saul by name (twice, in the Hebrew form), asking why he persecuted; Robertson notes the name repeated and the same question in each account; the personal, searching address. The witnesses press the searching question: "why persecutest thou me?" — the question identifies the persecution of the church with the persecution of Christ himself; Saul thought he persecuted only the followers of Jesus, but Christ says "me" — the Head identifying himself with his members; the union of Christ and his church; "inasmuch as ye have done it unto one of the least of these... ye have done it unto me" (Matthew 25:40). The expositors draw the lesson on the union of Christ and his church: in persecuting the church, Saul persecuted Christ; the Head feels the sufferings of his members; the believers one with Christ, their persecution his; the mystical union; the comfort that Christ feels his people's afflictions. The witnesses observe the personal, convicting nature of the question: "Saul, Saul" — the personal address, the name repeated; "why persecutest thou me?" — the convicting question, exposing the sin, turning the persecutor's act against himself; the searching word that pierced the conscience. The expositors note the grace in the question: Christ does not destroy the persecutor, but questions him; the question the beginning of the conversion; the grace that arrests and convicts, that it may save; the searching question the door to mercy. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's fall, the searching question, and the union of Christ and his church. The verse thus records Paul's fall and the voice — Saul, why persecutest thou me? — teaching the searching question that identified the persecution of the church with the persecution of Christ himself.
8:
"And I answered, Who art thou, Lord? And he said unto me, I am Jesus of Nazareth, whom thou persecutest." The witnesses note the dialogue and Christ's self-revelation. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "of Nazareth" (the Nazarene) is added here (not in Acts 9:5) because Jesus is mentioned now for the first time in the address; the form used also of his followers. The witnesses note Paul's question, "Who art thou, Lord?", and Christ's answer, "I am Jesus of Nazareth, whom thou persecutest" — the risen Christ revealing himself as the very Jesus whom Saul persecuted; the persecutor confronted with the One he opposed. The witnesses note the question. The witnesses note Christ's self-revelation.

The witnesses note Christ revealing himself — "I am Jesus of Nazareth, whom thou persecutest" — the persecutor confronted with the risen Lord.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's self-revelation. The lesson the expositors draw is the risen Christ revealing himself as the very Jesus whom Saul persecuted. The witnesses note Paul's question: "Who art thou, Lord?" — Paul, fallen and arrested, asks who is speaking; the persecutor seeking to know the One who addressed him; the question of the awakened conscience. The expositors observe Christ's answer: "I am Jesus of Nazareth, whom thou persecutest" — the risen Christ reveals himself as Jesus of Nazareth, the very One whom Saul persecuted; Robertson notes "of Nazareth" (the Nazarene) is added here because Jesus is named for the first time in the address; the self-revelation of the risen Lord. The witnesses press the confrontation: Saul, who thought Jesus dead and his cause to be crushed, is confronted with the living, risen Christ — "I am Jesus... whom thou persecutest"; the persecutor face to face with the One he opposed; the risen Lord, alive and glorified, the very Jesus he hated; the confrontation that shattered his unbelief. The expositors draw the lesson on the resurrection proved to Saul: the appearance proved that Jesus was risen, alive, glorified — not the dead impostor Saul supposed, but the living Lord; the resurrection demonstrated to the persecutor; the foundation of Paul's faith and apostleship laid in this appearance; he "seen of me also" (1 Corinthians 15:8). The witnesses observe the identification repeated: "whom thou persecutest" — again Christ identifies himself with the persecuted church; Saul's persecution of the Christians was persecution of Jesus; the union confirmed; the persecutor's sin against Christ himself. The expositors note the grace in the revelation: Christ reveals himself, not to destroy, but to convert; the self-revelation the means of Saul's conversion; the risen Lord meeting the persecutor with grace; the revelation that converts. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's question, Christ's self-revelation, and the persecutor confronted with the risen Lord. The verse thus records Christ's self-revelation — I am Jesus of Nazareth, whom thou persecutest — teaching the risen Christ revealing himself to the persecutor and the resurrection proved to Saul.
9:
"And they that were with me saw indeed the light, and were afraid; but they heard not the voice of him that spake to me." The witnesses note the experience of Paul's companions and the harmonizing with Acts 9. Ellicott explains "they heard not the voice" means they did not hear it as a voice uttering articulate words — it was for them as though it thundered (John 12:29); reconciling with Acts 9:7 (they heard "a voice/sound"). Robertson notes the accusative here (vs the genitive in Acts 9:7) indicates they did not understand what they heard. The witnesses note Paul's companions seeing the light and afraid, yet not hearing the articulate voice — the harmonization, they heard the sound but not the words. The witnesses note the companions' experience. The witnesses note the harmonization.

The witnesses note Paul's companions seeing the light but not hearing the articulate voice — the harmonization with Acts 9.

So the witnesses make the verse the companions' experience and the harmonization. The lesson the expositors draw is the reality of the appearance, attested by the companions, and the harmonizing of the accounts. The witnesses note the companions' experience: "they that were with me saw indeed the light, and were afraid; but they heard not the voice of him that spake to me" — Paul's companions saw the light and were afraid, but did not hear the articulate voice that spoke to Paul. The expositors address the apparent discrepancy with Acts 9:7 ("hearing a voice/sound, but seeing no man"): Ellicott explains "they heard not the voice" means they did not hear it as a voice uttering articulate words — it was for them as though it thundered (John 12:29, where the crowd heard a sound but thought it thunder); Robertson notes the accusative here (vs the genitive in Acts 9:7) indicates they did not understand what they heard; the harmonization — they heard the sound but not the articulate words; they heard a voice, but not the voice (the meaning). The witnesses press the reconciliation: in Acts 9:7, "hearing a voice" means hearing the sound; here, "heard not the voice" means not hearing the articulate words addressed to Paul; the two accounts harmonized — the companions heard the sound but not the message; no contradiction, but the distinction between hearing the sound and understanding the words. The expositors draw the lesson on the reality of the appearance: the companions saw the light and heard the sound — confirming the reality of the appearance; yet the message was for Paul alone — the personal call; the companions witnesses to the event, but the revelation Paul's own; the appearance real, the call personal. The witnesses observe the honest handling of the apparent discrepancy: the witnesses do not hide the difficulty, but show the harmonization; the distinction of hearing the sound and the words; the credibility of the accounts upheld; the difference reconciled. The expositors note the personal nature of the call: the companions saw and feared, but the voice spoke to Paul alone; the call personal, the revelation his; the grace addressed to the individual. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the companions' experience, the harmonization, and the reality of the appearance with the personal call. The verse thus records the companions' experience — seeing the light, not hearing the articulate voice — teaching the reality of the appearance and the harmonizing of the accounts.
10:
"And I said, What shall I do, Lord? And the Lord said unto me, Arise, and go into Damascus; and there it shall be told thee of all things which are appointed for thee to do." The witnesses note Paul's submissive question and Christ's direction. Calvin marks the submission: "this is the voice of a tamed man, and this is the true turning unto the Lord; when laying away all fierceness and fury, we bow down our necks willingly to be ruled." Robertson notes "are appointed for thee" (the perfect passive of tassō). The witnesses note Paul, converted, asking "What shall I do, Lord?" — the submissive question of the tamed persecutor; and Christ directing him to Damascus to learn what was appointed; the surrender and the direction. The witnesses note the submissive question. The witnesses note the direction to Damascus.

The witnesses note Paul's submissive question — "What shall I do, Lord?" — the true turning of the tamed persecutor.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's submissive question and Christ's direction. The lesson the expositors draw is the true conversion shown in the submissive question, and the direction to Damascus. The witnesses note the question: "What shall I do, Lord?" — Paul, converted, asks what he should do; the submissive question of the surrendered persecutor; the will brought to obedience; the man ready to do Christ's will. The expositors observe Calvin's marking of the submission: this is the voice of a tamed man, the true turning unto the Lord — when, laying away all fierceness and fury, we bow our necks willingly to be ruled; Paul's question the mark of genuine conversion, the surrender of the will, the readiness to obey; the tamed persecutor. The witnesses press the meaning of the submissive question: "What shall I do, Lord?" — the persecutor, lately breathing threatenings, now asks what he should do; the will surrendered, the heart turned, the man ready to obey; the true conversion in the submission; "Lord, what wilt thou have me to do?" (Acts 9:6). The expositors draw the lesson on the marks of true conversion: the surrender of the will, the readiness to obey, the question "what shall I do?"; the converted heart seeking to do Christ's will; the submission the fruit of conversion; the tamed will the mark of grace. The witnesses note Christ's direction: "Arise, and go into Damascus; and there it shall be told thee of all things which are appointed for thee to do" — Christ directs Paul to Damascus, where he would learn what was appointed; Robertson notes "are appointed" (the perfect passive of tassō); the things ordained for Paul to do; the direction through the appointed means (Ananias, v.12). The expositors observe the use of means in the direction: Christ does not tell Paul all at once, but directs him to Damascus, where Ananias would instruct him; the use of human means in the conversion; the Lord directing through his servants; the means appointed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's submissive question, the true turning of the tamed persecutor, and the direction to Damascus. The verse thus records Paul's submissive question and Christ's direction — what shall I do, Lord? go into Damascus — teaching the true conversion shown in the submissive question and the direction through the appointed means.
11:
"And when I could not see for the glory of that light, being led by the hand of them that were with me, I came into Damascus." The witnesses note Paul's blindness from the light. Gill notes the light, above the brightness of the sun, so dazzled his eyes that he could not see his way, and was led by the hand into the city. Ellicott marks the personal recollection — while Acts 9:8 states only the fact of blindness, Paul here connects it with its cause (the glory of the light). The witnesses note Paul, blinded by the glory of the light, led by the hand into Damascus — the proud persecutor humbled, led helpless into the city he came to ravage. The witnesses note the blindness. The witnesses note the persecutor led helpless.

The witnesses note Paul blinded by the glory and led by the hand — the proud persecutor humbled, led helpless into Damascus.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's blindness. The lesson the expositors draw is the humbling of the proud persecutor, blinded and led helpless into the city. The witnesses note the blindness: "when I could not see for the glory of that light" — the light, brighter than the sun, so dazzled Paul's eyes that he was struck blind; Ellicott marks the personal recollection — Paul connects the blindness with its cause (the glory of the light), where Acts 9:8 states only the fact; the persecutor blinded by the glory of Christ. The expositors observe the helpless leading: "being led by the hand of them that were with me, I came into Damascus" — Paul, blinded, was led by the hand into Damascus; Gill notes he could not see his way and was led; the helpless persecutor brought into the city. The witnesses press the humbling of the proud: Paul, who set out armed with letters to ravage the church at Damascus (v.5), entered the city blinded, helpless, led by the hand; the proud persecutor humbled; the one who came to bind led in like a child; the humbling of grace. The expositors draw the lesson on the humbling of the sinner: God humbled the proud persecutor — blinded, helpless, led by the hand; the pride brought low; the strong man weakened; the humbling that precedes the exalting; "before honour is humility" (Proverbs 15:33); the proud abased. The witnesses observe the contrast with his coming: Paul came to Damascus to ravage the church, armed and confident; he entered blinded, helpless, led; the contrast between his intent and his arrival; the persecutor's plans overthrown; the grace that turned his errand. The expositors note the spiritual blindness pictured: the physical blindness a figure of his spiritual state — blinded to the truth, now to be enlightened; the outward darkness the prelude to the inward light; the blindness that precedes the seeing; the figure of conversion. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's blindness, the humbling of the proud persecutor, and the helpless leading into Damascus. The verse thus records Paul's blindness — led by the hand into Damascus — teaching the humbling of the proud persecutor, blinded and led helpless into the city he came to ravage.
12:
"And one Ananias, a devout man according to the law, having a good report of all the Jews which dwelt there," The witnesses note Ananias's character, described to commend him to the Jews. Gill notes Paul describes Ananias as "a devout man according to the law" — a strict observer of the law of Moses, both moral and ceremonial, of holy life and conversation; this part of his character Paul chose to mention as recommending him to the Jews he addressed; for though a disciple, Ananias, like many believing Jews, strictly observed the rituals of the law. The witnesses note Ananias, a devout, law-observing believer, well reported of by the Jews — described so to commend him and the gospel to Paul's Jewish hearers. The witnesses note Ananias's character. The witnesses note the conciliatory description.

The witnesses note Ananias described as a devout, law-observing believer — to commend him and the gospel to the Jews.

So the witnesses make the verse Ananias's character. The lesson the expositors draw is the wise description of Ananias to commend him and the gospel to the Jewish hearers. The witnesses note the description: "Ananias, a devout man according to the law, having a good report of all the Jews which dwelt there" — Paul describes Ananias (the man God sent to him) as a devout, law-observing believer, well reported of by the Jews at Damascus; Gill notes he was a strict observer of the law, of holy life and conversation. The expositors observe the purpose of this description that Gill marks: Paul chose to mention Ananias's devotion to the law because it would commend him to the Jews he addressed; showing that the man who instructed and baptized him was no enemy of the law, but a devout, law-observing Jew, well reported of; the conciliatory description, disposing the Jews to credit the account. The witnesses press the wisdom of the conciliation: by describing Ananias as devout and law-observing, Paul shows the Jews that the gospel was not contrary to the law, nor were its agents enemies of Moses; the man who baptized him a devout Jew; the conciliation continued; the gospel commended. The expositors draw the lesson on the wisdom of meeting men where they are: Paul, to win the Jews, describes Ananias in terms they would respect; the wisdom that removes prejudice, commends the gospel by its agents; the prudence of the apostle; the becoming all things to all men (1 Corinthians 9:20). The witnesses observe the character of Ananias: a devout man, of good report, law-observing yet a believer; the godly character that commended the gospel; the believer of good repute even among the Jews; the witness of a holy life. The expositors note that many believing Jews still observed the law: Ananias, like many of the believing Jews, strictly observed the rituals of the law; the Jewish Christians not yet wholly free from the ceremonial; the historical fact of the law-observing Jewish believers; the transition not yet complete. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Ananias's character, the conciliatory description, and the wisdom of commending the gospel by its agents. The verse thus records Ananias's character — a devout, law-observing believer, well reported of — teaching the wise description that commended him and the gospel to the Jewish hearers.
13:
"Came unto me, and stood, and said unto me, Brother Saul, receive thy sight. And the same hour I looked up upon him." The witnesses note Ananias's tender greeting and Paul's restored sight. Gill notes Ananias came to him at the house of Judas in the street called Straight, stood by him, and addressed him. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the same word for "looked up" and "receive thy sight" (anablepō) — "I received my sight and looked upon him." The witnesses note Ananias greeting Paul tenderly as "Brother Saul" and bidding him receive his sight, and Paul's sight restored that same hour — the tender reception of the former persecutor and the miracle of restored sight. The witnesses note the tender greeting. The witnesses note the restored sight.

The witnesses note Ananias greeting Paul as "Brother Saul" and restoring his sight — the tender reception of the former persecutor.

So the witnesses make the verse Ananias's greeting and Paul's restored sight. The lesson the expositors draw is the tender reception of the former persecutor and the miracle of restored sight. The witnesses note the greeting: "Brother Saul, receive thy sight" — Ananias greets Paul tenderly as "Brother Saul"; Gill notes he came to him at the house of Judas in the street called Straight; the tender address to the former persecutor; the brotherly reception. The expositors observe the grace in the greeting: Ananias, a disciple, calls the former persecutor "Brother Saul"; the one who came to ravage the church received as a brother; the grace that embraces the converted persecutor; the love that receives the former enemy; the brotherly welcome. The witnesses press the restored sight: "receive thy sight. And the same hour I looked up upon him" — Ananias bids Paul receive his sight, and Paul's sight is restored that same hour; Robertson notes the same word for "looked up" and "receive thy sight"; the miracle of restored sight; the blindness removed. The expositors draw the lesson on the restored sight: the physical sight restored a figure of the spiritual enlightening; the blindness removed, the eyes opened; the outward miracle a sign of the inward; the conversion completed in the restored sight; the figure of enlightenment. The witnesses observe the use of the means: Christ had directed Paul to Damascus, where Ananias would tell him what to do (v.10); now Ananias comes, greets him, restores his sight; the Lord working through his servant; the means appointed; the human instrument used. The expositors note the tenderness of Ananias toward the persecutor: Ananias, who had feared Paul (Acts 9:13-14), now comes tenderly, calls him brother, restores his sight; the grace overcoming the fear; the love that receives the converted enemy; the tender reception. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Ananias's tender greeting, the restored sight, and the reception of the former persecutor as a brother. The verse thus records Ananias's greeting and Paul's restored sight — Brother Saul, receive thy sight — teaching the tender reception of the former persecutor and the miracle of restored sight, a figure of enlightenment.
14:
"And he said, The God of our fathers hath chosen thee, that thou shouldest know his will, and see that Just One, and shouldest hear the voice of his mouth." The witnesses note Ananias's declaration of Paul's calling. Gill notes "the God of our fathers hath chosen thee" — from eternity, in his purposes; or "taken thee into his hand" to form and qualify thee; designing both his call by grace and to apostleship; and Paul represents Ananias speaking of "the God of our fathers" to show the gospel was not contrary to the faith of Israel. Barnes notes the ends — to know his will, see the Just One (the Messiah), and hear his voice. The witnesses note Ananias declaring Paul chosen by the God of the fathers to know God's will, see the Just One, and hear his voice — the divine calling of Paul. The witnesses note Paul's calling. The witnesses note the God of the fathers.

The witnesses note Ananias declaring Paul chosen by the God of the fathers — to know God's will, see the Just One, and hear his voice.

So the witnesses make the verse Ananias's declaration of Paul's calling. The lesson the expositors draw is the divine election and calling of Paul, grounded in the God of the fathers. The witnesses note the choosing: "The God of our fathers hath chosen thee" — Ananias declares that God had chosen Paul; Gill notes this means God chose him from eternity, in his purposes, or took him into his hand to qualify him, designing his call by grace and to apostleship; the divine election of Paul. The expositors observe the appeal to "the God of our fathers": Paul represents Ananias speaking of "the God of our fathers" (the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob) to show that the gospel was not contrary to the faith of Israel, nor introduced a new God; the continuity of the gospel with the faith of the fathers; the conciliation continued. The witnesses press the ends of the choosing that Barnes names: "that thou shouldest know his will, and see that Just One, and shouldest hear the voice of his mouth" — Paul was chosen to know God's will (the plan of salvation), to see the Just One (the Messiah, Christ — whom he saw on the Damascus road), and to hear his voice; the threefold calling — to know, to see, to hear; the qualification for his apostleship. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine calling of Paul: God chose Paul, qualified him to know his will, see Christ, and hear his voice — that he might be his witness (v.15); the apostle divinely called and qualified; the calling grounded in God's choice; the election to service. The witnesses observe the title "that Just One": Christ called the Just One (the righteous, innocent One, Acts 3:14); Paul chosen to see him; the Messiah, the Holy and Just; the seeing of the risen Christ the ground of Paul's apostleship; he "seen of me also" (1 Corinthians 15:8). The expositors note the grounding in the God of the fathers: the calling of Paul was the act of the God of Israel; the gospel the fulfilment of the faith of the fathers; the continuity affirmed; no new God, but the God of Abraham calling Paul. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's calling, the divine choice, and the ends — to know, see, and hear. The verse thus records Ananias's declaration of Paul's calling — chosen to know God's will, see the Just One, and hear his voice — teaching the divine election and calling of Paul, grounded in the God of the fathers.
15:
"For thou shalt be his witness unto all men of what thou hast seen and heard." The witnesses note Paul's appointment as a witness to all men. Barnes notes Paul was to be a witness "to testify to all men that the Messiah has come, that he has died, that he has risen, and that he is the Saviour of the world"; of what he had seen and heard — the remarkable proof of Christ's divine mission. Ellicott notes this mission, identical with that of the Twelve (Acts 1:8), virtually placed Paul among the apostles. The witnesses note Paul appointed to be Christ's witness to all men — Jews and Gentiles — of what he had seen and heard; the apostolic commission to bear universal witness. The witnesses note the witness to all men. The witnesses note the apostolic commission.

The witnesses note Paul appointed Christ's witness to all men — Jews and Gentiles — of what he had seen and heard.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's appointment as a witness. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostolic commission of Paul to bear universal witness to Christ. The witnesses note the appointment: "thou shalt be his witness unto all men of what thou hast seen and heard" — Paul was appointed to be Christ's witness, to all men, of what he had seen and heard; Barnes notes he was to testify that the Messiah has come, died, risen, and is the Saviour of the world; the apostolic commission to witness. The expositors observe the universality of the witness: "unto all men" — Paul was to witness to all men, Jews and Gentiles; the universal commission; the witness not to the Jews only, but to all; the apostle to the Gentiles (v.21); the world-wide witness. The witnesses press the substance of the witness: "of what thou hast seen and heard" — Paul was to testify of what he had himself seen (the risen Christ) and heard (his voice); the eyewitness testimony; the apostle a witness of the risen Lord; "seen of me also" (1 Corinthians 15:8); the testimony grounded in his own experience. The expositors draw the lesson on the apostolic commission, which Ellicott marks: this mission, identical with that given to the Twelve (Acts 1:8, "ye shall be witnesses unto me"), virtually placed Paul among the apostles; the commission to witness the mark of the apostle; Paul an apostle, a witness of the risen Christ. The witnesses observe the ground of Paul's apostleship: he had seen and heard the risen Christ; the qualification for the witness; the apostle who had seen the Lord; the eyewitness commissioned to testify; the apostleship grounded in the seeing. The expositors note the office of witness: the apostle a witness — testifying of Christ, his death, resurrection, and salvation; the gospel the witness of the apostles; Paul's life given to this witness; the office of testimony. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's appointment as a witness, the universal commission, and the testimony of what he had seen and heard. The verse thus records Paul's appointment as Christ's witness — to all men, of what he had seen and heard — teaching the apostolic commission to bear universal witness to Christ.
16:
"And now why tarriest thou? arise, and be baptized, and wash away thy sins, calling on the name of the Lord." The witnesses note the call to baptism and the question of washing away sins. Gill presses against delay: though not Paul's case, many procrastinate obedience to baptism; but no delay ought to be made, it being Christ's command, to be complied with at once. On "wash away thy sins," the witnesses note the careful reading: baptism the sign and seal of the washing away of sins by the blood of Christ, "calling on the name of the Lord" the means — not baptism as the efficient cause of pardon (against baptismal regeneration), but the outward sign of the inward washing received by faith. The witnesses note the call to baptism. The witnesses note washing away sins.

The witnesses note the call to baptism — "wash away thy sins" — baptism the sign of the washing by Christ's blood, received by faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to baptism. The lesson the expositors draw is the duty of prompt obedience to baptism, and the careful understanding of "wash away thy sins." The witnesses note the call: "why tarriest thou? arise, and be baptized" — Ananias urges Paul not to delay, but to be baptized at once; Gill presses against delay — though not Paul's case, many procrastinate obedience to baptism, but no delay ought to be made, baptism being Christ's command, to be complied with at once; the call to prompt obedience. The expositors observe the duty of prompt obedience: baptism is Christ's command, the appointed confession of faith; the believer is to obey without delay; the procrastination that delays obedience reproved; the prompt obedience commended. The witnesses press the careful reading of "wash away thy sins, calling on the name of the Lord": the witnesses are careful to understand this rightly — baptism does not itself wash away sins (against baptismal regeneration), for sins are washed away by the blood of Christ, received by faith; rather, baptism is the sign and seal of the washing away of sins, and "calling on the name of the Lord" (faith in Christ) the means by which the washing is received; the baptism the outward sign of the inward washing. The expositors draw the lesson on baptism as a sign: baptism represents and seals the washing away of sins by the blood of Christ; the outward sign of the inward grace; not the efficient cause of pardon, but the sign and pledge; "the blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanseth us from all sin" (1 John 1:7); the washing by the blood, the sign in baptism. The witnesses observe the phrase "calling on the name of the Lord": the washing of sins is received by calling on the Lord — by faith, prayer, the invoking of Christ; the means of the washing the calling on Christ; faith the channel; the inward act joined to the outward sign. The expositors note the guard against superstition: the witnesses guard against making baptism the cause of pardon; the sins washed away by Christ's blood, received by faith (calling on his name); baptism the sign, not the substance; against baptismal regeneration. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the call to prompt baptism, baptism as the sign of the washing by Christ's blood, and the washing received by faith. The verse thus records the call to baptism — wash away thy sins, calling on the name of the Lord — teaching the duty of prompt obedience and baptism as the sign of the washing by Christ's blood, received by faith.
17:
"And it came to pass, that, when I was come again to Jerusalem, even while I prayed in the temple, I was in a trance;" The witnesses note Paul's vision in the temple. Chrysostom notes Paul thrusts himself into danger — coming to Jerusalem after the vision; and the witness follows from the result: Christ said "they will not receive thy testimony," and they did not; establishing both that the Jews were without excuse (persecuting him contrary to all reason) and that Christ was God (prophesying things contrary to expectation). The witnesses note Paul, returned to Jerusalem, praying in the temple, falling into a trance — the vision in the temple, the occasion of his commission to the Gentiles. The witnesses note the temple vision. The witnesses note the trance in prayer.

The witnesses note Paul's vision in the temple — praying, he fell into a trance, the occasion of his Gentile commission.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's vision in the temple. The lesson the expositors draw is the divine guidance given in prayer, the temple vision the occasion of the Gentile commission. The witnesses note the setting: "when I was come again to Jerusalem, even while I prayed in the temple, I was in a trance" — Paul, returned to Jerusalem, was praying in the temple when he fell into a trance and saw a vision; the revelation given in the season of prayer; the trance the means of the vision. The expositors observe the place: Paul received the vision in the temple — the very place the Jews now accused him of profaning (Acts 21:28); the irony that Paul, charged with despising the temple, had there received a vision from the Lord; the apostle a true worshipper at the temple; the conciliation continued. The witnesses press the trance in prayer: "while I prayed... I was in a trance" — the vision came while Paul prayed; the revelation given in the season of devotion; God meeting the praying apostle with the vision; the connection of prayer and revelation. The expositors draw out Chrysostom's observation: Paul thrusts himself into danger, coming to Jerusalem after the vision; and the witness follows from the result — Christ said "they will not receive thy testimony" (v.18), and they did not; establishing both that the Jews were without excuse (persecuting him contrary to all reason — for the former persecutor's testimony should have been the more credible) and that Christ was God (prophesying things contrary to expectation, foreknowing the future); the vision proving both the Jews' guilt and Christ's deity. The witnesses observe the purpose of the vision: the temple vision was the occasion of Paul's commission to the Gentiles (v.21); the Lord directing him from Jerusalem to the Gentiles; the vision the divine direction of his mission; the commission given. The expositors note the divine guidance in the vision: the Lord directs Paul's mission by the vision; the apostle led by divine revelation; the mission ordered by God; the guidance of the Spirit. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's vision in the temple, the revelation given in prayer, and the occasion of the Gentile commission. The verse thus records Paul's vision in the temple — praying, he fell into a trance — teaching the divine guidance given in prayer, the temple vision the occasion of his Gentile commission.
18:
"And saw him saying unto me, Make haste, and get thee quickly out of Jerusalem: for they will not receive thy testimony concerning me." The witnesses note Christ's direction to leave Jerusalem. Barnes notes Paul "saw him" — the Lord Jesus; "for they will not receive" — the inhabitants of Jerusalem, both Jews (who would hate him as an apostate) and Christians (who would remember his persecutions and be suspicious of him); Christ directing him to leave because his testimony would be rejected. The witnesses note the Lord directing Paul to hasten out of Jerusalem, for the people would not receive his testimony — the foreknowledge of Christ and the redirection of Paul's mission. The witnesses note the direction to leave. The witnesses note the rejected testimony.

The witnesses note Christ directing Paul to leave Jerusalem — for his testimony would be rejected; the foreknowledge of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's direction to leave Jerusalem. The lesson the expositors draw is the foreknowledge of Christ and the redirection of Paul's mission from the unreceptive Jerusalem. The witnesses note the direction: "Make haste, and get thee quickly out of Jerusalem" — the Lord, in the vision, directs Paul to leave Jerusalem at once; the urgent command to depart; the redirection of his mission. The expositors observe the reason: "for they will not receive thy testimony concerning me" — Barnes notes the inhabitants of Jerusalem, both Jews (who would hate Paul as an apostate) and Christians (who would remember his persecutions and be suspicious), would not receive his testimony; the rejection foreseen; the testimony to be refused. The witnesses press the foreknowledge of Christ: the Lord foreknew that Paul's testimony would be rejected at Jerusalem; the foreknowledge directing the redirection; Christ knowing the future, ordering Paul's mission accordingly; the divine foresight. The expositors draw the lesson on Christ's foreknowledge, which Chrysostom marked (v.17): Christ prophesied that they would not receive Paul's testimony, contrary to all expectation (for the former persecutor's witness should have been the more credible); and so it proved; the foreknowledge of Christ proving his deity; the prophecy fulfilled. The witnesses observe the redirection of the mission: Christ directs Paul from the unreceptive Jerusalem to the Gentiles (v.21); the mission redirected where it would be received; the providence ordering the field; the gospel sent where it would be welcomed. The expositors note the wisdom of the redirection: Paul, longing to win his own people (v.19-20), is directed away from them to the Gentiles; the wisdom that sends the gospel where it will be received; the providence overruling Paul's desire for the wider mission; the redirection for the gospel's advance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Christ's direction to leave Jerusalem, the foreknowledge of the rejection, and the redirection of the mission. The verse thus records Christ's direction to leave Jerusalem — for they will not receive thy testimony — teaching the foreknowledge of Christ and the redirection of Paul's mission from the unreceptive Jerusalem.
19:
"And I said, Lord, they know that I imprisoned and beat in every synagogue them that believed on thee:" The witnesses note Paul's protest, pleading his former persecution as a reason they should believe him. Barnes notes "they know" — the Christians know, and would therefore be unlikely to receive their former enemy; and that beating was often done in the synagogue (Matthew 10:17), the converts still meeting with the Jews. The witnesses note Paul, in the vision, protesting that the people knew he had imprisoned and beaten the believers — reasoning that his testimony should be the more credible, since the change in so great a persecutor was a proof of the truth. The witnesses note Paul's protest. The witnesses note the plea of his former persecution.

The witnesses note Paul protesting his former persecution as a reason they should believe — the change in the persecutor a proof.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's protest. The lesson the expositors draw is Paul's reasoning that his former persecution should make his testimony the more credible. The witnesses note the protest: "Lord, they know that I imprisoned and beat in every synagogue them that believed on thee" — Paul, in the vision, protests that the people knew he had imprisoned and beaten the believers; Barnes notes beating was often done in the synagogue (Matthew 10:17), the converts still meeting with the Jews; the plea of his former persecution. The expositors observe Paul's reasoning: he pleads his former persecution as a reason his testimony should be received — for the change in so great a persecutor, from imprisoning and beating the believers to preaching Christ, was a striking proof of the truth; the very man who had persecuted now testifying, his conversion the evidence; the persecutor's witness the more credible. The witnesses press the natural calculation: by human reason, the testimony of the former persecutor should have been the more readily received — for who could doubt the truth that converted such an enemy? Paul's protest the voice of this calculation; the surmise that they would believe. The expositors draw the lesson, with Chrysostom's observation (v.17): the natural calculation was that they would receive him — for he had been the persecutor, his conversion a proof; yet Christ foreknew they would not; the contrast between the human reasoning and the divine foreknowledge; the Jews persecuting him contrary to all likelihood, and so without excuse. The witnesses observe Paul's longing for his people: the protest reveals Paul's desire to win his own people; he would reason with them, plead his conversion, win them; the heart longing for their salvation (Romans 9:1-3); the apostle reluctant to leave them. The expositors note the submission that would follow: though Paul protests, he would submit to Christ's direction (v.21); the desire yielding to the command; the apostle obeying though he longed to stay; the submission to the divine will. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's protest, the plea of his former persecution, and the reasoning that his testimony should be credible. The verse thus records Paul's protest — they know I persecuted the believers — teaching his reasoning that the change in the persecutor should make his testimony the more credible.
20:
"And when the blood of thy martyr Stephen was shed, I also was standing by, and consenting unto his death, and kept the raiment of them that slew him." The witnesses note Paul's recollection of his part in Stephen's death. Gill notes Stephen was the proto-martyr, the first martyr who suffered for Christ, his blood shed by stoning. Barnes notes Paul was standing by, consenting to his death, and kept the raiment of those who slew him (Acts 7:58; 8:1) — showing that, though not stoning Stephen, he was with them in spirit. The witnesses note Paul recalling his part in Stephen's martyrdom — consenting, keeping the clothes — as further proof of his former enmity, pleading it as a reason his conversion should convince. The witnesses note Stephen's martyrdom. The witnesses note Paul's part in it.

The witnesses note Paul recalling his part in Stephen's death — consenting, keeping the clothes — the depth of his former enmity.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's recollection of his part in Stephen's death. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth of Paul's former enmity, pleaded as proof that his conversion should convince. The witnesses note the recollection: "when the blood of thy martyr Stephen was shed, I also was standing by, and consenting unto his death, and kept the raiment of them that slew him" — Paul recalls his part in Stephen's martyrdom; Gill notes Stephen was the proto-martyr, the first to suffer for Christ, stoned to death; Barnes notes Paul stood by, consented, and kept the clothes of those who slew him (Acts 7:58; 8:1). The expositors observe the significance: Paul, though he did not himself stone Stephen, was with the murderers in spirit — standing by, consenting, keeping their clothes; the guilt of the consenting, the partaker in the murder; the depth of his enmity shown in his part in the first martyrdom. The witnesses press the recollection of so great a sin: Paul remembers his part in the death of Stephen — the holy martyr; the weight of the memory; the consenting to the murder of a saint; the depth of his former wickedness; "I was standing by, and consenting." The expositors draw the lesson on the depth of Paul's former enmity: his part in Stephen's death the proof of how great a persecutor he had been; the consenting to the martyr's death; the foil to the grace that converted him; "the chief of sinners" (1 Timothy 1:15); the depth of the sin magnifying the grace. The witnesses observe the purpose in pleading it: Paul pleads his part in Stephen's death (as his other persecutions, v.19) as a reason his conversion should convince — for the change in one who had consented to the murder of the saints was a striking proof of the truth; the persecutor's witness the more credible; the plea continued. The expositors note the tenderness of the memory: Paul calls Stephen "thy martyr" — owning the saint he had helped to slay; the penitent's recollection; the memory of the sin against the martyr; the sorrow of the converted persecutor. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's part in Stephen's death, the depth of his enmity, and the plea that his conversion should convince. The verse thus records Paul's recollection of his part in Stephen's death — consenting, keeping the clothes — teaching the depth of his former enmity, pleaded as proof that his conversion should convince.
21:
"And he said unto me, Depart: for I will send thee far hence unto the Gentiles." The witnesses note Christ's commission of Paul to the Gentiles. Barnes notes Christ directed Paul to depart, because the Christians at Jerusalem would not receive him; "far hence" — Paul travelled far in the heathen nations, much of his ministry spent in distant regions. Clarke notes this was the particular appointment of Paul — the apostle of the Gentiles. The witnesses note the Lord commanding Paul to depart and sending him far away to the Gentiles — the great commission that defined Paul's apostleship; the word that would provoke the riot. The witnesses note the Gentile commission. The witnesses note the apostle to the Gentiles.

The witnesses note Christ commissioning Paul to the Gentiles — the great commission that defined his apostleship.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's commission of Paul to the Gentiles. The lesson the expositors draw is the great commission that defined Paul's apostleship, sending him far to the Gentiles. The witnesses note the command: "Depart: for I will send thee far hence unto the Gentiles" — Christ directs Paul to depart from Jerusalem and sends him far away to the Gentiles; Barnes notes "far hence" — Paul travelled far in the heathen nations, much of his ministry in distant regions; the commission to the Gentiles. The expositors observe Clarke's note on Paul's particular appointment: this was the particular appointment of Paul — the apostle of the Gentiles; though he preached to the Jews, his special commission was to the Gentiles, to carry the gospel to the nations; the apostleship defined; "the apostle of the Gentiles" (Romans 11:13). The witnesses press the significance of the commission: the word "Gentiles" defined Paul's mission — sent far hence to the nations; the gospel to the Gentiles the great theme of his apostleship; the commission that shaped his life and travels; the apostle to the Gentiles. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine commission: Paul was sent by Christ to the Gentiles; the mission not his choosing but Christ's command; the apostle commissioned by the Lord; the divine appointment of his field; "I will send thee." The witnesses observe the redirection from the Jews: Paul, longing to win his own people (v.19-20), is sent away from them to the Gentiles; the redirection of his mission; the desire yielding to the command; the wider field appointed; the providence ordering the Gentile mission. The expositors note the word that would provoke the riot: the word "Gentiles" would provoke the rage of the Jews (v.22); the mention of the Gentile commission the spark of the riot; the Jewish exclusivism inflamed; the word that broke the silence. The witnesses draw the lesson on the Gentile mission: the believer marks Christ's commission of Paul to the Gentiles, the great appointment, and the word that would provoke the riot. The verse thus records Christ's commission of Paul to the Gentiles — I will send thee far hence — teaching the great commission that defined Paul's apostleship, sending him to the nations.
22:
"And they gave him audience unto this word, and then lifted up their voices, and said, Away with such a fellow from the earth: for it is not fit that he should live." The witnesses note the riot provoked by the word "Gentiles." Gill notes the Jews heard him patiently until he mentioned his mission to the Gentiles; all the rest they either did not understand or looked on as an idle tale; but when he pretended to a divine mission to the Gentiles, this they could not bear. The witnesses note the Jews, who had listened patiently, erupting in rage at the word "Gentiles" — crying for Paul's death; the Jewish exclusivism inflamed, unable to bear that the Gentiles should be admitted to equal privilege. The witnesses note the riot. The witnesses note the Jewish exclusivism.

The witnesses note the Jews erupting at the word "Gentiles" — crying for Paul's death; the Jewish exclusivism inflamed.

So the witnesses make the verse the riot provoked by the word "Gentiles." The lesson the expositors draw is the Jewish exclusivism, unable to bear that the Gentiles should be admitted to equal privilege. The witnesses note the patient hearing until the word: "they gave him audience unto this word" — the Jews had listened patiently to Paul's account (his Jewish credentials, his conversion); Gill notes they heard him until he mentioned his mission to the Gentiles; all the rest they bore, but at this they erupted; the silence broken by the word "Gentiles." The expositors observe the eruption: "then lifted up their voices, and said, Away with such a fellow from the earth: for it is not fit that he should live" — at the word "Gentiles," the Jews erupted in rage, crying for Paul's death; the fury unleashed; the demand that he be killed; "away with such a fellow." The witnesses press the cause of the rage: the word "Gentiles" — the Jews could not bear that the Gentiles should be admitted to equal privilege with themselves; their exclusivism inflamed; the pride that held the Gentiles unworthy; the rage at the Gentile mission. The expositors draw the lesson on the Jewish exclusivism: the Jews, proud of their privilege, could not endure that the Gentiles should be received; the exclusivism that grudged the gospel to the nations; the rage at the breaking down of the middle wall; the pride that hated the Gentile mission; "forbidding us to speak to the Gentiles that they might be saved" (1 Thessalonians 2:16). The witnesses observe the irony of the demand: "it is not fit that he should live" — the Jews, who professed zeal for God, demand the death of the apostle for preaching the gospel to the Gentiles; the murderous rage; the zeal without knowledge (Romans 10:2); the demand for blood. The expositors note the providence in the eruption: the riot, provoked by the word "Gentiles," would lead to Paul's protection by the Romans (v.24-29) and his journey to Rome; the providence overruling the rage; the riot the occasion of Paul's preservation and the gospel's advance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the riot at the word "Gentiles," the Jewish exclusivism, and the rage at the Gentile mission. The verse thus records the riot provoked by the word "Gentiles" — away with such a fellow — teaching the Jewish exclusivism, unable to bear that the Gentiles should be admitted to equal privilege.
23:
"And as they cried out, and cast off their clothes, and threw dust into the air," The witnesses note the violent expressions of the mob's rage. Barnes notes they cast off their outer garments, probably intending to stone Paul (as Acts 7:58); and threw dust into the air as expressive of their abhorrence and indignation — a striking exhibition of rage and vindictive malice; Paul being guarded by Roman soldiers, their only way of expressing wrath was by these tokens (as Shimei against David, 2 Samuel 16:13). The witnesses note the mob casting off their clothes and throwing dust — the violent, frenzied expressions of their rage against Paul, unable to reach him; the impotent fury of the inflamed multitude. The witnesses note the casting off of clothes. The witnesses note the frenzied rage.

The witnesses note the mob casting off clothes and throwing dust — the frenzied, impotent expressions of their rage against Paul.

So the witnesses make the verse the violent expressions of the mob's rage. The lesson the expositors draw is the frenzied, impotent fury of the inflamed multitude against Paul. The witnesses note the actions: "as they cried out, and cast off their clothes, and threw dust into the air" — the mob cried out, cast off their outer garments, and threw dust into the air; Barnes notes the casting off of clothes was probably the preparation to stone Paul (as at Stephen's death, Acts 7:58); the throwing of dust an expression of abhorrence and indignation; the violent tokens of rage. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the rage: these were a striking exhibition of rage and vindictive malice; Paul being guarded by Roman soldiers, the mob could not reach him, so their only way of expressing wrath was by these tokens — the casting off of clothes, the throwing of dust (as Shimei cursed and threw dust at David, 2 Samuel 16:13); the impotent fury. The witnesses press the frenzy: the mob, unable to reach Paul (guarded by the soldiers), vented their rage in violent gestures — crying, casting off clothes, throwing dust; the frenzied, impotent fury; the rage that could not strike, only gesture; the inflamed multitude. The expositors draw the lesson on the impotence of the rage: the mob's fury was impotent — they could not reach Paul, guarded by the soldiers; their rage spent in gestures; the providence restraining their violence; the fury that could not harm; the rage bounded. The witnesses observe the providence in the soldiers' guard: Paul, in the midst of the murderous mob, was guarded by the Roman soldiers; the providence protecting him through the very Romans; the fury restrained; the apostle preserved; the providence using the civil power. The expositors note the picture of the inflamed multitude: the casting off of clothes, the throwing of dust, the crying out — the frenzied gestures of the mob; the picture of rage without reason; the violence of the inflamed; the irrational fury. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the mob casting off clothes and throwing dust, the frenzied rage, and the impotent fury restrained by providence. The verse thus records the violent expressions of the mob's rage — casting off clothes, throwing dust — teaching the frenzied, impotent fury of the inflamed multitude, restrained by providence.
24:
"The chief captain commanded him to be brought into the castle, and bade that he should be examined by scourging; that he might know wherefore they cried so against him." The witnesses note the chief captain's order to examine Paul by scourging. Gill notes the captain brought Paul into the castle to save him from the rage of the people and to examine him privately, though by a wrong and unjustifiable method. Barnes notes "the castle" was the tower of Antonia; "examined" (the word applied to testing metals by fire) means to torture to extract the truth. The witnesses note the chief captain ordering Paul scourged to extract the cause of the uproar — the unjust method of torture to learn the truth; the Roman ignorance of the case. The witnesses note the order to scourge. The witnesses note the unjust examination.

The witnesses note the chief captain ordering Paul scourged to extract the cause — the unjust method of torture.

So the witnesses make the verse the order to examine Paul by scourging. The lesson the expositors draw is the unjust method of torture to extract the truth, the Roman ignorance of the case. The witnesses note the bringing into the castle: "the chief captain commanded him to be brought into the castle" — the captain brought Paul into the castle (Barnes notes the tower of Antonia); Gill notes this was to save him from the rage of the people and to examine him privately; the apostle removed from the mob. The expositors observe the order to scourge: "bade that he should be examined by scourging; that he might know wherefore they cried so against him" — the captain ordered Paul scourged to extract the cause of the uproar; Barnes notes "examined" (the word for testing metals by fire) means to torture to extract the truth; the unjust method. The witnesses press the injustice of the method: the captain, not understanding the case (the speech being in Hebrew), resorted to torture to learn why the mob raged; the scourging to extract the truth; the cruel, unjust method; Gill notes it was a wrong and unjustifiable way. The expositors draw the lesson on the Roman ignorance and injustice: the captain, ignorant of the case, would torture Paul to learn it; the unjust resort to scourging; the cruelty of extracting truth by torture; the injustice of the method, contrary even to Roman law (the scourging of a citizen, v.25). The witnesses observe the providence even here: the order to scourge would be the occasion of Paul's asserting his Roman citizenship (v.25), which would protect him and advance the gospel; the providence overruling the injustice; the unjust order turned to Paul's protection. The expositors note the contrast with the apostle's calm: Paul, about to be scourged, calmly asserts his rights (v.25); the prudence of the apostle; the calm in the face of the injustice; the wise use of his citizenship. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the order to scourge, the unjust method of torture, and the providence overruling for Paul's protection. The verse thus records the order to examine Paul by scourging — to extract the cause — teaching the unjust method of torture and the providence overruling for Paul's protection.
25:
"And as they bound him with thongs, Paul said unto the centurion that stood by, Is it lawful for you to scourge a man that is a Roman, and uncondemned?" The witnesses note Paul's assertion of his Roman citizenship. Gill notes they bound him with thongs to a pillar to be scourged, after the Roman manner; and Paul, before the scourging, asks the centurion whether it was lawful to scourge a Roman, and uncondemned. The witnesses note Paul, bound for scourging, asserting his Roman citizenship — the lawful use of his civil rights to stay the injustice; the scourging of an uncondemned Roman being a grave violation of the law. The witnesses note the assertion of citizenship. The witnesses note the lawful claim.

The witnesses note Paul asserting his Roman citizenship — staying the unjust scourging by the lawful use of his rights.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's assertion of his Roman citizenship. The lesson the expositors draw is the lawful use of civil rights to stay injustice. The witnesses note the moment: "as they bound him with thongs" — Paul was bound to a pillar to be scourged, after the Roman manner (Gill notes); the preparation for the scourging; the apostle about to be tortured.
