The expositors observe the assertion: "Paul said unto the centurion that stood by, Is it lawful for you to scourge a man that is a Roman, and uncondemned?" — Paul, before the scourging, asks the centurion whether it was lawful to scourge a Roman citizen, uncondemned; the assertion of his Roman citizenship; the lawful claim. The witnesses press the grounds of the claim: to scourge a Roman citizen was forbidden by law; and to scourge one uncondemned (without trial) was a further violation; Paul, a Roman citizen, uncondemned, could not lawfully be scourged; the double illegality; the lawful claim to stay the injustice. The expositors draw the lesson on the lawful use of civil rights: Paul, who had borne the mob's rage patiently, now asserts his Roman citizenship to stay the unjust scourging; the lawful use of his rights; not from fear or cowardice, but to prevent an illegal act and to protect himself for further service; the right use of citizenship (as Acts 16:37). The witnesses observe the timing of the assertion: Paul asserts his citizenship before the scourging, at the right moment; the prudence that uses the lawful means at the right time; the wise assertion; the calm claim in the face of the injustice. The expositors note the balance in Paul: he bore the injustice of the mob, but lawfully resists the illegal scourging; the patience that endures and the prudence that asserts rights; not contradictory, but the wise use of lawful means; the believer free to use his civil rights. The witnesses observe the effect this would have: the assertion would alarm the centurion and the captain (v.26-29), staying the scourging; the lawful claim effectual; the injustice prevented; the apostle protected. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's assertion of his citizenship, the lawful use of his rights, and the staying of the injustice. The verse thus records Paul's assertion of his Roman citizenship — is it lawful to scourge a Roman, uncondemned? — teaching the lawful use of civil rights to stay injustice.
26:
"When the centurion heard that, he went and told the chief captain, saying, Take heed what thou doest: for this man is a Roman." The witnesses note the centurion's alarm and warning. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the better reading gives "What art thou about to do?" — a sharp warning. Calvin observes that some marvel the centurion so credulously affirmed the thing, as if he knew it true; but their manner was that one who claimed Roman citizenship, unless he could prove it, was punished; Paul's claim taken seriously. The witnesses note the centurion, alarmed at Paul's claim, hastening to warn the chief captain — "take heed what thou doest, for this man is a Roman"; the gravity of the violation feared. The witnesses note the centurion's alarm. The witnesses note the warning.

The witnesses note the centurion alarmed at Paul's claim — warning the captain, "this man is a Roman"; the gravity feared.

So the witnesses make the verse the centurion's alarm. The lesson the expositors draw is the gravity of the violation feared, the centurion hastening to warn the captain. The witnesses note the alarm: "when the centurion heard that, he went and told the chief captain" — the centurion, hearing Paul's claim to Roman citizenship, was alarmed and hastened to warn the chief captain; the seriousness of the claim recognized; the centurion's haste. The expositors observe the warning: "Take heed what thou doest: for this man is a Roman" — Robertson notes the better reading, "What art thou about to do?"; a sharp warning; the centurion warning the captain of the danger of scourging a Roman citizen; the gravity of the violation. The witnesses press the seriousness of the claim that Calvin notes: some marvel the centurion so readily affirmed it; but their manner was that one who claimed Roman citizenship was taken seriously, and if he could not prove it, was punished; the claim not lightly made or received; the centurion crediting it. The expositors draw the lesson on the gravity of the violation: to scourge a Roman citizen was a grave crime, severely punished; the centurion and captain in danger if they proceeded; the fear of the violation staying their hand; the seriousness of the Roman law. The witnesses observe the providence in the centurion's alarm: the centurion's haste to warn the captain stayed the scourging; the providence using the centurion's alarm to protect Paul; the lawful claim effectual; the apostle preserved. The expositors note the respect for Roman citizenship: the centurion and captain, on hearing the claim, were alarmed; the high regard for the rights of a Roman citizen; the law protecting the citizen; the gravity of violating it. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the centurion's alarm, the warning to the captain, and the gravity of the violation feared. The verse thus records the centurion's alarm — take heed, this man is a Roman — teaching the gravity of the violation feared and the providence staying the scourging.
27:
"Then the chief captain came, and said unto him, Tell me, art thou a Roman? He said, Yea." The witnesses note the captain's question and Paul's confirmation. Ellicott notes the pronoun is emphatic: "Thou, the Jew speaking both Greek and Hebrew, art thou a citizen of Rome?" — the combination of so many discordant elements was almost incredible. Robertson notes "Thou (emphatic) a Roman? It was unbelievable." The witnesses note the chief captain, surprised, asking Paul directly whether he was a Roman, and Paul simply confirming it — the apostle's claim, surprising to the captain, plainly affirmed. The witnesses note the captain's question. The witnesses note Paul's confirmation.

The witnesses note the captain incredulously asking whether Paul was a Roman — and Paul plainly confirming it.

So the witnesses make the verse the captain's question and Paul's confirmation. The lesson the expositors draw is the surprise of the captain at Paul's claim and the plain affirmation of it. The witnesses note the question: "Then the chief captain came, and said unto him, Tell me, art thou a Roman?" — the captain, hearing the centurion's report, came himself to ask Paul directly whether he was a Roman; the question asked in person; the captain's concern. The expositors observe the incredulity Ellicott and Robertson mark: the pronoun is emphatic — "Thou, the Jew speaking both Greek and Hebrew, art thou a citizen of Rome?"; the combination of so many discordant elements (a Jew, speaking Greek and Hebrew, yet a Roman citizen) was almost incredible; the captain's surprise; "it was unbelievable." The witnesses press the captain's surprise: that Paul — a Jew, lately the object of the mob's rage, seemingly a common man — should be a Roman citizen was hard to believe; the captain's incredulity; the unexpected claim. The expositors draw the lesson on the apostle's standing: Paul, though a Jew and lately mobbed, was a Roman citizen — of higher standing than the captain supposed; the apostle of no mean position; the citizenship that surprised the captain. The witnesses observe Paul's plain confirmation: "He said, Yea" — Paul simply, plainly affirms his citizenship; no elaboration, no boasting; the calm, plain claim; the truth affirmed. The expositors note the calm of the apostle: Paul, in the face of the captain's surprise, simply confirms the truth; the calm, dignified affirmation; the apostle at ease; the plain claim. The witnesses observe the effect of the confirmation: Paul's confirmation would lead the captain to fear and to release him (v.29); the plain claim effectual; the injustice stayed; the apostle protected. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the captain's incredulous question, Paul's plain confirmation, and the surprise at the apostle's standing. The verse thus records the captain's question and Paul's confirmation — art thou a Roman? Yea — teaching the surprise of the captain at Paul's claim and the plain affirmation of it.
28:
"And the chief captain answered, With a great sum obtained I this freedom. And Paul said, But I was free born." The witnesses note the captain's purchased citizenship and Paul's freeborn status. Gill notes the captain was not a Roman born, but very likely a Grecian or Syrian (by his name Lysias); and as all things were now venal at Rome, the freedom of the city was bought with money; he wondered that so mean a person as Paul should have such a privilege. Barnes notes the privilege of citizenship might be purchased. The witnesses note the captain saying he had bought his freedom at a great sum, and Paul replying that he was freeborn — Paul's citizenship by birth, higher than the captain's by purchase; the apostle's superior standing. The witnesses note the purchased citizenship. The witnesses note Paul freeborn.

The witnesses note the captain's purchased citizenship and Paul's freeborn status — the apostle's citizenship by birth, higher than the captain's.

So the witnesses make the verse the captain's purchased citizenship and Paul's freeborn status. The lesson the expositors draw is Paul's superior standing, a citizen by birth, higher than the captain's by purchase. The witnesses note the captain's claim: "With a great sum obtained I this freedom" — the captain says he had bought his Roman citizenship at a great sum; Gill notes he was not a Roman born, but likely a Grecian or Syrian (by his name Lysias), and as all was venal at Rome, the freedom was bought with money; the captain's citizenship purchased. The expositors observe Paul's reply: "But I was free born" — Paul replies that he was freeborn, a Roman citizen by birth; his citizenship inherited, not bought; the apostle freeborn; the higher standing. The witnesses press the contrast: the captain bought his citizenship at a great sum; Paul was freeborn, a citizen by birth; the apostle's citizenship superior to the captain's; the freeborn higher than the purchased; the contrast of standing. The expositors draw the lesson on Paul's superior standing: Paul, freeborn, was of higher standing than the captain, who had bought his freedom; the apostle of no mean position; the citizenship by birth honourable; the freeborn status. The witnesses observe the captain's surprise: Gill notes the captain wondered that so mean a person as Paul (a Jew, lately mobbed) should have such a privilege, and freeborn at that; the surprise at Paul's standing; the apostle of higher rank than supposed. The expositors note the providence in Paul's citizenship: Paul, born a Roman citizen (at Tarsus), had this privilege by birth; the providence giving him the standing that would protect him and serve the gospel; the citizenship a means of his preservation and mission; the providence in his birth. The witnesses observe the use of the citizenship: Paul's freeborn citizenship would protect him from the scourging and serve his appeals (to Caesar, Acts 25:11); the providence giving him the standing for his work; the citizenship serving the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the captain's purchased citizenship, Paul's freeborn status, and the providence in his standing. The verse thus records the captain's purchased citizenship and Paul's freeborn status — but I was free born — teaching Paul's superior standing, a citizen by birth, and the providence in his citizenship.
29:
"Then straightway they departed from him which should have examined him: and the chief captain also was afraid, after he knew that he was a Roman, and because he had bound him." The witnesses note the departure of the soldiers and the captain's fear. Barnes notes they immediately departed, seeing that by scourging him they would have violated the Roman law and exposed themselves to its penalty; the captain also was afraid, having bound a Roman citizen. The witnesses note the soldiers, who would have scourged Paul, departing at once, and the captain afraid for having bound a Roman — the injustice stayed, the apostle protected by his citizenship; the fear of the Roman law. The witnesses note the departure. The witnesses note the captain's fear.

The witnesses note the soldiers departing and the captain afraid — the injustice stayed, Paul protected by his citizenship.

So the witnesses make the verse the departure of the soldiers and the captain's fear. The lesson the expositors draw is the staying of the injustice and the protection of Paul by his citizenship. The witnesses note the departure: "straightway they departed from him which should have examined him" — the soldiers who would have scourged Paul departed at once; Barnes notes they saw that by scourging him they would have violated the Roman law and exposed themselves to its penalty; the scourging stayed; the soldiers withdrawing. The expositors observe the captain's fear: "the chief captain also was afraid, after he knew that he was a Roman, and because he had bound him" — the captain was afraid, having bound a Roman citizen (even the binding being a violation); the fear of the Roman law; the captain in danger for his act. The witnesses press the staying of the injustice: the scourging was stayed, the soldiers departed, the captain afraid; the injustice prevented by Paul's citizenship; the lawful claim effectual; the apostle protected. The expositors draw the lesson on the protection of Paul: Paul, about to be unjustly scourged, was protected by his Roman citizenship; the lawful means staying the injustice; the providence using the citizenship to preserve him; the apostle delivered. The witnesses observe the fear of the Roman law: the captain and soldiers feared the law, which severely punished the violation of a citizen's rights; the high regard for the citizen's rights; the law protecting Paul; the fear of the penalty. The expositors note the providence in the deliverance: Paul, in danger of the unjust scourging, was delivered by his citizenship and the fear it produced; the providence preserving the apostle; the citizenship a means of his protection; the deliverance for further service. The witnesses observe the reversal: the captain, who would have scourged Paul, now afraid for having bound him; the tables turned; the apostle, lately to be tortured, now in the position of advantage; the reversal by his citizenship. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the departure of the soldiers, the captain's fear, and the protection of Paul by his citizenship. The verse thus records the departure of the soldiers and the captain's fear — afraid for having bound a Roman — teaching the staying of the injustice and the protection of Paul by his citizenship.
30:
"On the morrow, because he would have known the certainty wherefore he was accused of the Jews, he loosed him from his bands, and commanded the chief priests and all their council to appear, and brought Paul down, and set him before them." The witnesses note the captain bringing Paul before the Sanhedrin. Gill notes Paul was kept in the castle all night; the captain would know the certainty of the accusation, which he could not yet learn, some saying one thing and some another. Barnes notes he summoned the sanhedrim, being prevented from scourging Paul, to learn from the Jews the nature of the charge. The witnesses note the captain, to learn the true cause of the accusation, loosing Paul and bringing him before the Sanhedrin — the providence ordering Paul's appearance before the council; the close of the chapter. The witnesses note the appearance before the council. The witnesses note the search for the charge.

The witnesses note the captain bringing Paul before the Sanhedrin — to learn the charge; the providence ordering his appearance.

So the witnesses make the verse the captain bringing Paul before the Sanhedrin. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence ordering Paul's appearance before the council to learn the charge. The witnesses note the captain's purpose: "because he would have known the certainty wherefore he was accused of the Jews" — the captain wished to learn the true cause of the accusation; Gill notes he could not yet learn it, some saying one thing and some another; the search for the charge. The expositors observe the action: "he loosed him from his bands, and commanded the chief priests and all their council to appear, and brought Paul down, and set him before them" — the captain loosed Paul (the binding being unlawful), summoned the sanhedrim, and set Paul before them; Barnes notes he did this, being prevented from scourging Paul, to learn from the Jews the nature of the charge; the apostle brought before the council. The witnesses press the captain's difficulty: the captain could not learn the cause from the mob (some crying one thing, some another, v.24); so he brings Paul before the Sanhedrin, that the Jews themselves might state the charge; the search for the truth; the lawful inquiry. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence ordering Paul's appearance: the captain, to learn the charge, brings Paul before the Sanhedrin; the providence ordering the apostle's appearance before the council; the occasion of the defence to come (Acts 23); the providence directing the events. The witnesses observe the loosing of Paul: the captain loosed Paul from his bands; the unlawful binding undone; the apostle freed from the bands; the injustice further corrected; the citizen's rights respected. The expositors note the providence in the whole sequence: the mob's rage, the order to scourge, Paul's citizenship, the appearance before the council — all ordered by providence; the apostle preserved, the gospel advanced, Paul brought toward Rome (Acts 23:11); the providence directing every step. The witnesses observe the close of the chapter: the chapter ends with Paul set before the Sanhedrin; the stage set for his defence before the council; the providence ordering the next scene; the apostle preserved and brought forward. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the captain bringing Paul before the Sanhedrin, the search for the charge, and the providence ordering his appearance. The verse thus records the captain bringing Paul before the Sanhedrin — to learn the charge — teaching the providence ordering Paul's appearance before the council and the apostle preserved for the defence to come.
